questions.question: What is the difference between rumination and productive reflection?
questions.answer: Rumination is repetitive, passive focus on problems without seeking solutions — 'Why does this always happen to me?' It increases anxiety and depression. Productive reflection is curious, non-judgmental examination with the goal of learning — 'What happened? What can I learn? What will I do differently?' The key difference is orientation: rumination looks backward with judgment; reflection looks forward with curiosity. Self-compassion is the differentiator — research shows self-compassionate reflection leads to growth, while self-critical reflection leads to rumination.

questions.question: Explain Gibbs' Reflective Cycle and why each stage matters.
questions.answer: Gibbs' six-stage cycle: (1) Description — what happened (facts only); (2) Feelings — what you thought and felt (honesty); (3) Evaluation — what was good and bad (judgment); (4) Analysis — what sense you can make (understanding); (5) Conclusion — what else you could have done (learning); (6) Action Plan — what you'll do next time (application). Each stage matters because most people skip from 'what happened' to 'what I should do' without the analysis that makes action effective. The feelings stage is crucial because emotions often drive behavior — ignoring them means missing root causes.

questions.question: What does research say about the relationship between reflection and performance improvement?
questions.answer: Research shows employees who spend 15 minutes reflecting at the end of the day performed 23% better than those who didn't. A Harvard Business School study found that workers who reflected for just 10 minutes at the end of each day learned 22% faster than those who didn't reflect. Reflection works because it consolidates learning, extracts patterns, and updates mental models. Without reflection, you can have 20 years of experience or 1 year repeated 20 times. The key is structured reflection with action steps — unstructured reflection can devolve into rumination.

questions.question: How can someone start a journaling habit that actually sticks?
questions.answer: Five evidence-based strategies: (1) Start tiny — 2 minutes per day minimum. Consistency matters more than length. (2) Attach to an existing habit — journal right after your morning coffee or before bed. (3) Use prompts rather than free-writing — 'What drained my energy today?' is easier than 'write about your day.' (4) Don't judge your entries — messy, incomplete, and honest is better than polished and inauthentic. (5) Review weekly — look for patterns. Research shows the habit forms in about 66 days on average. The most common failure mode is starting too ambitiously (30-minute sessions) and burning out.

questions.question: How does the Johari Window framework apply to self-reflection?
questions.answer: The Johari Window has four quadrants: Open (known to self and others), Blind Spot (unknown to self, known to others), Hidden (known to self, unknown to others), and Unknown (unknown to both). Self-reflection can expand the Open Area in two ways: (1) Self-disclosure — sharing Hidden Area information with others (appropriate vulnerability builds trust), (2) Feedback seeking — asking others about your Blind Spot ('What's one thing you see in me that I might miss?'). Monthly, ask yourself: 'What do I know about myself that I haven't shared?' Quarterly, ask trusted colleagues for blind spot feedback. This creates a virtuous cycle of increasing self-knowledge.
